title: GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
GDP per capita, 2020
This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The choropleth map displays global GDP per capita for the year 2020, adjusted for inflation and living costs, using a color scale that ranges from light green for the lowest values to dark blue for the highest. The legend defines specific bands starting at $0 and extending past $20,000, with intermediate thresholds at $1,000, $2,000, $5,000, and $10,000. The highest values, represented by the darkest blue shade, are concentrated in North America, Western Europe, and parts of Oceania. The United States, Canada, Australia, and Norway all fall into this top bracket, indicating a GDP per capita exceeding $20,000. Western European nations such as Germany, France, and the United Kingdom also appear in this highest category. Moving down the scale, countries in South America like Brazil and Argentina, along with Russia and China, occupy the middle bands, generally falling between $5,000 and $10,000 or slightly below. Mexico and much of Eastern Europe sit in the $5,000 to $10,000 range. The lowest values, shown in the lightest green shades corresponding to the $0 to $2,000 bands, dominate the African continent and parts of South Asia. Nations such as Niger, Chad, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo are visible in these lowest categories. India appears in a light green band, placing it in the lower-middle range relative to the global distribution. Greenland and several smaller island nations are marked with hatching to indicate no data is available. The visual distribution shows a clear geographic clustering where the highest economic output per person aligns with North America and Western Europe, while the lowest output aligns with sub-Saharan Africa.
